Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Oon Swee Gek

Facts.Text: She was one of the claimants who incorporated Violet Oon Inc Pte Ltd in Singapore on 2012-03-26
She served as the company's Culinary Curator and Chef, and she was one of its directors

On 2014-11-13, she was one of the claimants who entered into the 2014 shareholders' agreement and subscription agreement with MMM Delicious, which subscribed for 50% of the company's shares for $450,000
Following the investment, her personal shareholding was reduced to 20% of the company's total shares
On 2019-02-21, she was among the claimants who agreed to Mr Murjani's list of 2019 Terms

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a founding shareholder is entitled to statutory relief for minority oppression if corporate restructuring agreements were extracted through economic duress
Party_Details.Rule: A shareholder may petition the court for a buyout or to set aside agreements if another shareholder leverages illegitimate commercial pressure and undue influence to commandeer the company
Party_Details.Application: The 2019 agreements were extracted through economic duress and undue influence after the co-investor manufactured a financial crisis to commandeer control of the company.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the 2019 agreements were executed under economic duress and undue influence. The judge set aside the agreements and ordered a buyout of the Defendants' shares under Section 216 of the Companies Act to resolve the oppression.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Tay Su-Lyn

Facts.Text: Tay Su-Lyn was one of the company's three directors when it was incorporated on 2012-03-26
Tay Su-Lyn was employed as the Company's Creative Director, Marketing Manager, and Events & Catering Manager, and she was responsible for branding, business development, and event planning
Tay Su-Lyn met Mr Murjani only once on 22 or 23 February 2019, and he otherwise refused to speak to her directly
She executed the revised 2019 Shareholders' Agreement

Party_Details.Issue: The issue was whether the 2019 Agreements could be avoided for economic duress or undue influence
Party_Details.Rule: The party relying on economic duress must show pressure directed at compulsion of the will, illegitimate pressure, and that but for the pressure the agreement would not have been made
Party_Details.Application: The 2019 agreements should be set aside because they were procured by illegitimate commercial pressure, economic duress, and undue influence.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court agreed that the pressure applied was illegitimate and that the directors were subjected to undue influence. The agreements were set aside.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Tay Yiming

Facts.Text: On 2012-03-26, Tay Yiming was one of the claimants who incorporated the Company and served as a director
In February 2019, Mr Murjani dealt mainly with Tay Yiming and refused to speak to Ms Oon or Ms Tay except for one meeting
On 2019-02-26, Tay Yiming executed the revised shareholders' agreement

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the 2019 Agreements, entered into after a dispute over salary increases, were procured by economic duress or undue influence
Party_Details.Rule: A shareholder may establish oppression if they are unfairly excluded from the management of a company in contravention of an underlying mutual understanding of shared control
Party_Details.Application: The co-investor illegitimately leveraged a salary dispute and isolated family members to exert economic duress and undue influence to seize corporate control.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court reasoned that Mr Murjani used the salary dispute to pressure Tay Yiming, exaggerated the claimed salary overpayments and his own contributions, and deliberately isolated him as part of a strategy to secure control of the Company.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Violet Oon Inc Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: The company was incorporated in Singapore on 2012-03-26
On 2014-11-13, MMM Delicious Private Limited subscribed for 50% of the company's shares for $450,000, leaving the shareholding split 20%, 15%, 15%, and 50% among the claimants and MMM Delicious
Around 2020-03-01, the company faced cash flow issues during the COVID-19 pandemic, and the parties agreed that the claimants' salaries would be withheld and accumulated as debt owed by the company
On 2019-02-26, the claimants and Group MMM executed a revised shareholders' agreement that altered the company's board composition and management arrangements

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a corporate entity should be subject to a buyout order when its shareholders are deadlocked due to oppressive conduct by one faction
Party_Details.Rule: When a company's affairs are conducted in a manner that is commercially unfair to a shareholder, the court may intervene to regulate the company's affairs or order the purchase of shares to break the deadlock
Party_Details.Application: As the nominal subject of the dispute, the company was merely the vehicle through which the shareholder deadlock and oppressive conduct manifested.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: As the nominal subject of the dispute, the court ordered that the shares in the company held by the Defendants be bought out by the Claimants to resolve the oppressive deadlock.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Murjani Manoj Mohan

Facts.Text: Murjani Manoj Mohan, through his wholly-owned corporate vehicle MMM Delicious Private Limited, subscribed to 50% of Violet Oon Inc Pte Ltd's shares under the 2014 SHA
Group MMM appointed Murjani Manoj Mohan as a director of Violet Oon Inc Pte Ltd
Murjani Manoj Mohan discovered the claimants' salary increases and raised the issue with them
Murjani Manoj Mohan emailed Mr Tay a list of proposed terms for the 2019 Agreements
Murjani Manoj Mohan and the claimants executed the revised shareholders' agreement, the 2019 SHA, on behalf of Group MMM

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director is legally responsible for minority oppression after utilizing economic duress to force the execution of revised corporate agreements
Party_Details.Rule: Corporate agreements may be set aside, and oppression remedies triggered, if a party leverages illegitimate economic pressure and undue influence to seize control of a company
Party_Details.Application: The 2019 agreements were standard, freely negotiated commercial rescue measures necessary to save the business, completely devoid of economic duress or undue influence.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that Murjani Manoj Mohan exerted economic duress and undue influence to change the shareholder arrangements with a view to taking control of the Company. The court ordered the Claimants to buy out the Defendants' shares at fair value.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Group MMM Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Group MMM Pte Ltd is an investment vehicle entirely owned and controlled by Murjani Manoj Mohan
The corporate vehicle executed an agreement to acquire 50% of the shares in Violet Oon Inc Pte Ltd
The vehicle provided various short-term financial advances to the company to cover operational shortfalls
Under the disputed 2019 agreements, the vehicle was granted enhanced veto rights and a more favorable debt repayment structure

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the 2019 Agreements were procured by economic duress or undue influence
Party_Details.Rule: Section 216 of the Companies Act provides a personal remedy where commercial unfairness is shown, assessed against the parties' commercial agreement and legitimate expectations
Party_Details.Application: The revised agreements reflected valid commercial negotiations and legitimate agreements between shareholders, not oppressive conduct or illegitimate pressure.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that Mr Murjani exerted economic duress and undue influence to change the shareholder arrangements, set aside the 2019 Agreements, and ordered the claimants to buy Group MMM's shares at fair value.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Oon Swee Gek

Facts.Text: Oon Swee Gek is a founding shareholder holding a 20% stake in the food and beverage company
Following the breakdown of the 2019 agreements, trust completely eroded between the founding family and the majority investor

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company should be ordered to wind up on just and equitable grounds due to an irretrievable breakdown of trust between its shareholders
Party_Details.Rule: A court may order the winding up of a company if there is a complete deadlock in management or a fundamental breakdown of the mutual trust required to operate the business
Party_Details.Application: The irretrievable breakdown of mutual trust and complete management deadlock necessitates the winding up of the company on just and equitable grounds.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: While the court recognized the irretrievable breakdown of trust, it determined that winding up a successful business was an unnecessarily destructive remedy. Because the court ordered a buyout remedy under the oppression claim (OC 301), it formally dismissed the winding up application.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Violet Oon Inc Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Violet Oon Inc Pte Ltd is a profitable operational company employing numerous staff across several restaurant outlets
Despite the shareholder dispute, the company remained a going concern with ongoing commercial operations

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a financially viable operational company should be liquidated to resolve an unworkable shareholder deadlock
Party_Details.Rule: Courts view winding up as a remedy of last resort and will generally prefer to order a share buyout if the company remains a viable, profitable enterprise
Party_Details.Application: The company remains a financially viable and profitable ongoing enterprise, making liquidation an unnecessarily destructive and disproportionate remedy.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court declined to wind up the company. The judge concluded that ordering a buyout was a far more appropriate and proportionate remedy that preserved the enterprise value of the company while curing the deadlock.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Tay Su-Lyn

Facts.Text: The individual is a shareholder and director responsible for the brand's creative direction and marketing.
She encountered an irretrievable breakdown in communication with the majority investor, leading to a management impasse.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a shareholder is entitled to a winding-up order on just and equitable grounds when an alternative buyout remedy is available.
Party_Details.Rule: A winding-up petition will be refused if the court finds that a share purchase order can adequately resolve the deadlock without destroying the business.
Party_Details.Application: The Plaintiff sought winding up to exit the deadlock, but the court favored a buyout as a less destructive solution.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court dismissed the petition. While trust had failed, the entity remained a successful enterprise, making winding up a disproportionate and unnecessary remedy.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Tay Yiming

Facts.Text: The individual is a shareholder and director who managed the company's daily operations during the dispute.
He was the primary point of contact for the majority investor during the negotiations for the revised management terms.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a shareholder is entitled to a winding-up order when a less destructive remedy like a buyout is available to resolve a management deadlock.
Party_Details.Rule: A court will dismiss a winding-up application if a share purchase order under the oppression regime provides a more appropriate and proportionate remedy for the parties.
Party_Details.Application: The claimant sought winding up on just and equitable grounds, but the court determined that the buyout ordered in the related oppression suit was a superior solution.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court dismissed the winding-up application as it was deemed unnecessary given the successful outcome of the oppression claim, which allowed the company to continue as a going concern.
